main_summary.summary: Betty Garman Robinson, born January 8, 1939 in New York City, was interviewed by Emilye Crosby on December 8, 2015 in Baltimore for the Civil Rights History Project. Robinson traces her trajectory from a middle-class upbringing in Pleasantville, New York, where her physicist father worked at the MIT Radiation Lab and later General Precision Equipment, through Skidmore College, into the US National Student Association (NSA), where she became a top officer. She describes how the 1958 NSA Student Congress at Ohio Wesleyan radicalized her, leading to a 1960 picket at the Saratoga Springs Woolworth's in support of the Southern sit-ins. After graduation she participated in the International Student Relations Seminar (ISRS), where she came to understand the CIA's covert funding of NSA's international program. She joined SDS briefly before moving to SNCC's Atlanta office in March 1964 as a fundraising staffer, then became Northern Coordinator in fall 1964, served in Greenwood during Freedom Summer 1964, and transitioned to federal programs work in late 1965, documenting school desegregation noncompliance with Marion Barry at the US Office of Education. She joined the DC SNCC office for the Free DC voting-rights campaign and the 1966 bus boycott before resigning over internal culture. Robinson recounts her post-SNCC anti-war organizing, factory work in Baltimore from 1972, and her current racial-justice organizing with SURJ in solidarity with Black Lives Matter.
key_themes: National Student Association and CIA covert funding
federal programs and school desegregation enforcement
continuity from civil rights to Black Lives Matter
main_summary.historical_significance: Robinson's testimony is a rare first-person account of SNCC's Atlanta headquarters operations from the perspective of a white woman who moved from logistical support into senior coordination roles. Her detailed recall of the Northern Coordinator function (daily WATS-line calls to projects, donor thank-you letters, Friends of SNCC coordination, the Sojourner Motor Fleet, chartering a plane to Selma after the Edmund Pettus Bridge attack) documents the rarely-narrated administrative substrate that sustained field organizing in Mississippi, Alabama, and Arkansas. Her account of the National Student Association's covert CIA funding through the International Student Relations Seminar — including the "witting" code word and the practice of recruiting student travelers as informants on emerging foreign student leaders — is a primary-source confirmation of the 1967 Ramparts exposure as experienced by a participant. Her trajectory from SNCC through anti-war and labor organizing to present-day SURJ work makes her testimony a longitudinal case study in lifelong white anti-racist commitment.

chapters.title: Family and Childhood Formation
chapters.summary: Robinson describes her 1939 birth in New York City to a physicist father from a Pennsylvania-German farm family and a working-class New York mother. She recounts wartime years in Cambridge, Massachusetts, where her father worked at MIT's Radiation Lab on radar during the atomic bomb era, and her father's tortured experience at General Precision Equipment in suburban Pleasantville, New York, which shaped her rejection of corporate America.

chapters.title: Mother's Prejudice and Race Awakening
chapters.summary: Robinson recounts confronting her mother's racism after hosting an interracial YMCA-camp counselor party at their home, including her mother's slur upon discovering an African American Vassar student had been a guest. She describes her Mexican godmother Lucy Droz, her mother's anti-immigrant and anti-communist views, and her own gradual recognition that the absence of Black people from her high school and community was itself a structural fact she had not noticed.

chapters.title: Skidmore and the National Student Association
chapters.summary: Robinson enters Skidmore College in 1956 and is recruited to run for National Student Association (NSA) campus secretary, leading her to the 1958 NSA Student Congress at Ohio Wesleyan — a transformative encounter with students discussing South African apartheid, Southern segregation, and political organizing. She switches her major to political science and is elected NSA campus chair, then engages in the campaign against the National Defense Education Act loyalty oath, which she had studied in a civil-liberties course.
related_events: National Defense Education Act loyalty oath campaign

chapters.title: Saratoga Sit-In Solidarity 1960
chapters.summary: Following the February 1960 Greensboro sit-ins, Robinson organizes a Skidmore mass meeting that decides to march 200 women students around the Saratoga Springs Woolworth's and to picket the next day. The first four pickets are taken to the Saratoga police station; the local paper editorializes that the students were "unladylike." Robinson considers attending the SNCC founding conference at Shaw University over spring break but is deterred by her mother's anger over her recent letter to the Hastings-on-Hudson newspaper.

chapters.title: ISRS and CIA Covert Funding
chapters.summary: After graduating in 1960, Robinson attends the International Student Relations Seminar (ISRS) in Philadelphia, which she later learned was the training ground for NSA's CIA-funded international program. She describes the recruitment of "witting" officers, the practice of student travelers gathering intelligence on emerging foreign student leaders, and the maneuvering that kept Tim Jenkins (a Black student leader) out of the international vice presidency. She references Karen Paget's book Patriotic Betrayal as documenting this history.

chapters.title: SDS, Helsinki, and the Turn to SNCC
chapters.summary: Robinson serves on the SDS executive committee and travels to the 1962 World Festival of Youth and Students in Helsinki under CIA-funded auspices alongside Gloria Steinem and Harold Bakken. She witnesses Helsinki police beating no-testing East-West demonstrators. After the LID-SDS split meeting in New York, she concludes she is a wallflower in SDS's male intellectual culture and accepts James Forman and Casey Hayden's affirmation, moving toward SNCC where she can take concrete initiative.
keywords: World Festival of Youth and Students
related_events: 1962 World Festival of Youth and Students (Helsinki)

chapters.title: SNCC Atlanta Office and Northern Coordinator
chapters.summary: Robinson joins SNCC's Atlanta office on Raymond Street in March 1964 and becomes Northern Coordinator in fall 1964 after Dinky Romilly returns to New York. She describes the daily WATS-line calls to all SNCC projects, the use of operator numbers to dodge phone-company charges, fundraising letter campaigns (rejecting paternalistic appeals in favor of democratic-ideals framing), and her interracial housing on West Anthony Street with Joyce Brown, Carol Merritt, and Shirley Cooks. She earned $9.64 weekly after taxes in the field, raised to $40 in Atlanta.

chapters.title: Freedom Summer and Federal Programs
chapters.summary: Robinson serves as lead Northern Coordinator in the Greenwood, Mississippi SNCC office during Freedom Summer 1964. Her parents send an air conditioner while also pressuring her to come home. She canvasses with white volunteer Peter Orris and witnesses the MFDP convention challenge fallout. In fall 1965, she transitions to federal programs work, documenting discriminatory administration of ASCS agricultural subsidies, food stamps, and Civil Rights Act of 1964 school desegregation plans across Arkansas and DC. With Marion Barry she audits the US Office of Education's files and publishes a report exposing dispensations granted to Southern school districts.

chapters.title: Selma, Waveland, and Women in SNCC
chapters.summary: Robinson describes chartering a plane from Atlanta to Selma after the Edmund Pettus Bridge attack, sending Ivanhoe Donaldson, Courtland Cox, Prathia Hall, and a fourth staffer to back up John Lewis and John Love. She narrates SNCC's purchase of the Sojourner Motor Fleet at UAW-discounted prices and SNCC's internal soul-searching about strategy after the 1964 convention. She recalls the Waveland meetings of fall 1964 and the position paper on women by Mary King and Casey Hayden, noting her own non-engagement with women's liberation until the early 1970s because she felt affirmed within SNCC.
related_events: Bloody Sunday at the Edmund Pettus Bridge (March 7, 1965)

chapters.title: Washington DC SNCC and Free DC
chapters.summary: Robinson moves to the DC SNCC office on Rhode Island Avenue in late 1965, working alongside Marion Barry as he becomes head of the office. She participates in the Free DC voting-representation campaign, which pressures Woodward & Lothrop, Hecht Company, and other businesses to display the Free DC decal. She describes the divisive influence of L.D. Pratt, an older white advisor close to Barry, and a 1966 bus boycott on Benning Road organized via SNCC's basement phone bank. She resigns from SNCC by letter, citing the loss of participatory decision-making in the DC office.

chapters.title: Race, Gender, and Post-SNCC Years
chapters.summary: Robinson reflects on SNCC's interracial dynamics, including Black-only social spaces, the backsliding conversations at Waveland targeting Black men in interracial relationships (including her own with Marion Barry and Stokely Carmichael's), and being struck in the face by a Black youth in DC after Dr. King's 1968 assassination. She narrates her post-SNCC work: a Black community arts program in DC, anti-war organizing, consciousness-raising groups in the early 1970s, and her 1972 move to Baltimore to work in a factory (eight years), marriage to a Black factory worker, and birth of biracial daughters.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr. (April 1968)

chapters.title: SURJ, Black Lives Matter, and Movement Continuity
chapters.summary: Robinson narrates her late-career organizing: HRSA-funded medical-student factory tours, 14 years at Johns Hopkins School of Public Health in injury prevention and HIV research, and lead-organizer work with Citizens Planning and Housing Association in Baltimore. She joined SURJ (Showing Up for Racial Justice) after the 2015 Baltimore uprising following Freddie Gray's death, working in accountability relationships with Black Lives Matter organizations. She closes with reflections on movement timing, the centrality of Black liberation as the foundation for all other liberation struggles, and the importance of scholarship by Emilye Crosby and Hasan Kwame Jeffries.
related_events: Killing of Michael Brown in Ferguson (August 2014)
